sentences.text: My name is Maurice Raynor, which is spelled M-A-U-R-I-CE R-A-Y-N-O-R.
My birth name is Maurice Rabbie.
And my last name was spelled R-A-B-B-I-E.
I was born on the 15th of March, 1932, in Amsterdam, Holland.
My first recollection of Amsterdam is when I was quite small.
[PAUSES] Those recollections are where I lived at that time, before the war, before the Second World War, and from then on onwards.

In around about 1935, I would say, three or four years old, [PAUSES] we lived-- my father and mother and my sister-- we lived in an apartment above an apartment where lived my father's mother and father.
In other words, my grandparents on my father's side.
We lived on a street named Nieuwe Achtergracht, in center of Amsterdam.
And it's a street which still exists today.
And it has a canal, as so many have in Holland.
Around the corner of that street [PAUSES] was obviously another street, and it was called the Lepelstraat, which is spelled L-E-P-E-L Street.
And there lived my father's sister, only sister, known as Auntie Jet, Tante Jet, which comes from the word Henrietta.
She was married to my uncle by marriage, and his name was Emmanuel Fontaine.
They had a son, my cousin, younger than I, whose name was Siegfried, Siegfried Fontaine Rabbie.
[PAUSES] Although he was younger than myself, we did play together somewhat.
Down the canal street where I lived was another aunt of mine, who was the mother of one of my father's first cousins.
[PAUSES] His name was Abraham, or in Dutch, we called him Brahm Rabbie.
He was one of twins.
And he had a daughter, Phoebe Rabbie, my age.
And with her I used to play.
And at that time, of course, also my sister.
Normally, Friday night we-- that is, my mother and father, my sister, would spend Friday night with my grandparents.
And very often my Aunt Jet and my cousin Siegfried would then also be Friday nights at my grandparents.
Yes.
My father's father, name was Levi Rabbie.
And my grandmother's name was Leentje, which is the diminutive which we use in Dutch for Lena, or Helena, or Helen.
I remember my grandfather somewhat more than my grandmother, although I do remember my grandmother.
My grandmother was round, so to speak, wore spectacles, as did my grandfather.
My grandfather had always struck me at that time, at a very young age, beautiful, gray hair, white, wavy hair, as indeed had my father.
And I remember my grandfather taking me to shul, to the Ashkenazi Shul, which today is the museum, the Jewish Museum in Amsterdam.
I remember having Hanukkah there.
And I remember going on the high holy holidays to shul with my grandfather.
Yes.
My grandfather was a diamond trader on the diamond bourse, because my father's family were all in the diamond business, as were so many.
Most of the diamond business in Amsterdam before the war was Jewish people.
My father's name was Isaac Rabbie.
I remember of my father more a time after we had moved from that address where we lived before the war.
We moved to another address before the war.
And I remember my father, more from that time, that he used to take me fishing.
I remember that, of course, very fondly, and of course, with a great amount of sadness.
[PAUSES] My father would take me to meet his friends who had sons of my own age.
And I remember that.
And we used to bicycle together.
He'd either take me on his bicycle, which is, of course, very typical in Holland.
Still is today.

And when I was a little bit bigger, he bought me a secondhand bicycle, which he-- they had to elevate the pedals so that my feet could reach the pedals, because at that time, my legs weren't long enough.
And they couldn't lower the saddle of the bicycle.
I remember that.
No, my sister never did go fishing with me.
She stayed more with my mother.
It was more-- as I look upon it now, so many years after the event, it's like it was more a boys' thing to do.
It wasn't a thing where girls would be included.
My mother's name in English is Cissie, spelled C-I-S-S-I-E.
But in Dutch, it is Sientje, which is an old fashioned Dutch name, also used in the diminutive for the name Sien.
Her name in Dutch is spelled S-I-E-N-T-J-E.
No.
My mother was not working, only looking after the family, so to speak.
Yes.
[COUGHS]
Mostly games like hide and seek, things like that.
Or when we would get a little bit older, skipping with a rope.
Or a game which I don't know the name of today, where you with chalk make marks on the-- on the pavement and you kind of-- I don't know what it's called, hop, skip, and jump, or something like that.
I-- I remember-- I remember that kind of game.
My-- my-- my grandfather would make the kiddish.
And we'd all sit around.
And my grandmother would have baked her own bread.
And I always remember, one of the things I remember so well about my father's mother, my grandmother, that she used to cut the bread holding it against her bosom, cutting it like that.
It always-- that's always stuck in my mind as-- in today's world-- as that being so strange, that people would have cut bread that way.
But that was certainly the way that always stood out in my mind.
[PAUSES] And we'd be-- you know, we'd play after the kiddish.
And we'd eat.
You know, children, us children would play while the parents sat and talked about whatever [PAUSES] was being spoken about those days, which I'm not aware of of that time.
My mother-- my mother had the candles at home, but when we had the kiddish, we were normally by my grandparents, who had the candles lit.
Yes, I did.
I attended what is in English called, I suppose, primary school.
In Dutch, it would be called kleuterskool.
I attended that, which was an ordinary school, a government school.
But I also went to Jewish school for [NON-ENGLISH], which was just Jewish children.
Yes, I did.
More so when we had moved to one of the newer suburb of Amsterdam.
There I had a good friend, a non-Jewish friend, who-- with whom I went to school when I had moved to a different school, a higher-grade school.
Yes.
But in the early years, I didn't have any non-Jewish friends.
Yes.
Because of the part that we lived in Amsterdam, on that canal, there was a whole new area built.
And [PAUSES] although I don't know the exact reasons, I would assume [PAUSES] that my parents wanted to be in a newer area with more modern housing and newer schools.
I was approximately-- [PAUSES] I would say I was approximately 10 years old. 9 or 10.
Yes.
Obviously, although I'm speaking English, all my previous life has been in Dutch.
[PAUSES] I knew that [PAUSES] we weren't allowed to have Christmas trees, which we used to see as children in people's windows.
Because in Holland, people have their windows open and have their lights on in the evening.
And people are able to look in.
People don't close their windows in Holland.
They don't to this day.
So, of course, as children, especially around Christmas time, we would see Christmas trees lit.
And in those days, with candles, not with electric bulbs.
So I remember very distinctly asking my parents, you know, why didn't we have a Christmas tree?
You know, my friend from down the road had a Christmas tree.
Why haven't we got a Christmas tree?
And you know, my mother would say to me, well, we're Jews, and we don't have a Christmas tree.
We have Hanukkah lights.
And those are things I remember.
And I was very aware that I was not the same.
I knew that I wasn't Christian.
That I was Jewish.
And that we believed in the Old Testament.
We didn't believe in Jesus Christ.
[PAUSES] We believed the Moses and the Ten Commandments and tried to live our lives according to the Ten Commandments.
And that family was of very great importance to us.
And [PAUSES] you know, that we had our holidays, Purim and Simchat Torah, and nice things, instead of Christmas.
Only in so far-- only in so far that [PAUSES] I was not like he.
[PAUSES] He was not like me.
But we were like each other, because we were friends, and we were in Holland [PAUSES] living a normal life at that time.
[PAUSES] I knew there were differences.
I've always known there were differences, not so much, at that time, of things that might have been said.
[PAUSES] You know, playing with other children, non-Jewish children.
You know, I may have occasionally heard the name Jood, Jew in English.
[PAUSES] But nothing to any extent, at that time, at my age then, that-- [PAUSES] I noticed it, but it never aggravated me at that time.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
Not that I recall.
You mean amongst the Jewish friends, did we talk?
No, I was really-- I can't say.
I was fairly young in those days to discuss things.
It was more, you know, playful.
[PAUSES] Discussion wasn't part of my life at that age.
[PAUSES] We were more interested in play.

[SIGHS] I think the time that it started to impact me more than it had done [PAUSES] was when my mother and father started to talk [PAUSES] to me and in general about Jewish people that they knew, friends or people they heard about after-- this was after Holland capitulated in the war in May 1945, [PAUSES] when it surrendered to Germany.
My mother and father talking about Jewish people who had gone to England.
Had gotten out of the country in time, before all the borders were closed.
It is at that time that it started to impact me an awful lot that people were running away and getting out.
And of course, we were left.
Yes.
Not when I started school, but certainly after the occupation of Holland in 1940.
[PAUSES] I certainly remember the name Hitler, [PAUSES] because I started to hear it.
No.
No.

My relatives, [PAUSES] all my [PAUSES] ancestors, as far as I can ascertain, and I've been able to do that through the Dutch archives in Amsterdam, from approximately 1723, [PAUSES] my ancestors all lived in Holland, both my ancestors on my mother's side and on my father's side.
[PAUSES] The family that we had, that I had personally, was in Holland, my father's side, and in England, my mother's side.
Because my mother's parents and grandmother and that part of the family had left Holland at the turn of the century, in the early 1900s.
They had left Holland and settled in England.
And as a matter of fact, my mother and father were first cousins and have the same surname, last name.
My mother's maiden name is Rabbie.
My mother's married name is Rabbie.
They were first cousins.
So the family that I knew and had was half in Holland and half in England.

Well, as a matter of fact, my sister Helen and I used to spend every other summer in England to visit my mother's mother, my grandmother; and my mother's grandmother, my great grandmother; and my mother's brothers and sisters and cousins who lived in England.
In 1939, [PAUSES] my sister Helen and I were on such a visit to my grandparents.
To my grandmother.
I didn't know my grandfather, my mother's father, as he had died in 1918 in England.
We were on such a visit in the summer holidays in 1939.
[PAUSES] It was time for our visit to end, because we had to go back to school.
And my grandmother, my mother's mother, brought my sister and I back to Amsterdam, because that is where we lived.

[PAUSES] While my grandmother was there in August, visiting my mother and one of my mother's sisters, who had also married a Dutch man and who lived in Holland, my grandmother was notified by the British embassy in Amsterdam that she should immediately return to England because of there was a danger of war.
[PAUSES] My mother-- my grandmother asked my mother, my father, and of course, the two of us, to come with her to England.
And my father refused to go to England with my grandmother, as he did not want to leave his parents and his brother and sister and their families.
My grandmother then asked my mother, would she please let us children, my sister and I, go with my grandmother to England.
And my father said-- this is according to my mother-- my father said, where I go, my children go.
And my grandmother [PAUSES] left about a week before September 1939, when war was declared by Britain against Germany because of the invasion of Poland.
That was the last time-- although we didn't know it at the time-- that any of us saw my grandmother, my English grandmother.
She returned to England, and we stayed in Holland.
[PAUSES]
So my whole early life was spent on a boat between Holland and England.
And my mother's brothers would visit us in Holland, or vice versa.
So I've always been like between one country and another, because England was as much as my family as Holland was, although at that time, I didn't speak any English.
My grandmother could still speak a bit of Dutch.
And my great grandmother, who we called-- we named Granny Cluck, because that was her-- that was her name, [PAUSES] she spoke Dutch with me, because that's all I could speak at that time.
Yes, after the summer holidays, like is normal in Holland, we just went back to school.
[PAUSES] This was around the beginning of September 1939.
School holidays would have been over, because they're summer holidays.
And we would return to school normal.
To school.
No.
Not really.
I remember being aware that people would sit around the radio listening to the news and that there was fighting.
[PAUSES] Now I probably would imagine it was in Poland.
But that's the only change that I remember, really and truly, at that time.
And that was, of course, before Holland was occupied.
Not before the war so much.

But, of course, once that Holland was occupied, I noticed changes in the-- in the house, because of my parents talking about people that had left, that had gotten out, that had been able to pay to get out, to get on fishing boats across the North Sea to get-- to get to England.
And people had gone to Switzerland, [PAUSES] who knew the right people or were able to pay the right people the amounts required to get out and leave Holland.
Around that time, of course, when those things were happening, I was aware that things were changing.
Well, I remember the occupation.
I remember the Germans arriving very near to where I lived.
[PAUSES] I remember that.

And then, of course, slowly I remembered that I was told that there had been, in different parts of Amsterdam, what they call razzias, where the Germans would block off streets, where you couldn't get out.
I never-- I never experienced that personally.
They'd block off the streets, and everybody that was in there would have to be showing their papers.
Of course, at that time, as the occupation progressed, we had to wear a star.
The yellow star.
Where my grandparents lived, they had to put up boards at certain streets, saying Judenviertel, in Dutch-- in German, and Joodevijk, which is-- that's Dutch translation.
[PAUSES] And we all wore the star.
What's that?
Ah, Judenviertel means Jewish quarter.
Jewish area.
[PAUSES] That happened where my grandparents lived, in the area where we used to live.
But where we lived at that time, we didn't have such an area for a while.
Yes.
I can't really and truly recall that my mother sat my sister and I down and said, from tomorrow, we all have to wear the yellow star.
I can't remember her saying that.
She may well have done that.
All I remember is that they were on my jacket, or my coat, a star like that.
We got the star-- [PAUSES] I would say, approximately a year after the occupation of Holland.
Probably around about 1941.
And I would have been nine.
No.
No, I do not.
No, I do not.

The only-- I remember one particular occasion when one friend of mine-- obviously, we were all about the same age-- [PAUSES] said to me-- and although it's very young-- I remember him saying in Dutch-- and I will tell you the Dutch.
I will translate it for you.
[SPEAKING DUTCH], meaning, I knew you were a Jew.
And that-- that sort of [PAUSES] made me kind of inwardly jump.
But apart from that, no, I don't remember anybody pointing their finger, that I noticed, at me at that time.
No.
[SIGHS] I-- I felt no different than before, except that I was somewhat more aware of being different.
Even more intensified [PAUSES] from the time I wore the star.
And then, of course, the intensity got much worse as the time progressed.
No.
By that time, I was not going to the Jewish school anymore, because the Jewish school was in the old part of Amsterdam, where we had lived before.
And my father was afraid to take me there because of the razzias.
[PAUSES] In case we got stopped.
Because a lot of people were picked up on the razzias and disappeared.
So my father was afraid to take me.
OK.
Can I get up?
Can I disconnect?
